summary: McKenzie explains the fundamentals of stock exchange mechanics and algorithmic trading for generalist programmers, using Stockfighter (Starfighter's programming game) as the vehicle. He walks through order books, limit orders, market makers, spreads, liquidity, and risk management, then describes how Stockfighter lets developers practice these concepts via a REST API without risking real money. The post doubles as both a trading primer and a product pitch for Starfighter's hiring-through-gameplay business model. He argues that most web developers underestimate their ability to tackle problems like algorithmic trading, and that the barrier is psychological rather than technical.
key_points: Stock exchanges are built around order books—prioritized queues where bids and asks cross to create trades—and the core algorithm is surprisingly simple to implement
Market makers provide liquidity (time travel and teleportation as a service) by taking on price risk, and the spread is their compensation for that risk
HFT firms are mostly just market makers competing on speed, which benefits retail investors by compressing spreads down to a penny
Most developers believe algorithmic trading is beyond them, but a basic market maker can be written in ~144 lines of Ruby
Starfighter's business model is using programming games to identify talented engineers and connect them with employers
money_quotes: Every great developer you know got there by solving problems they were unqualified to solve until they actually did it.
I have a sneaking suspicion Wall Street invented many of these words to give customers the impression 'This is all really, really difficult — pay us money and the complexity goes away.'
A market maker's job is TTTaaS: Teleportation and Time Travel as a Service.
Bad news: teleportation and time travel aren't actually possible. This means you take on risk.
I ran the math and, on my portfolio of ~$80,000, I've paid approximately $6 to market makers over the last ten years.
Liquidity really does exist primarily because market makers are actively adding it.
The market doesn't believe there exist enough engineers who thrive when given a novel, hard problem. We think you're out there, in multitudes.
You can drive a car with your feet if you want to, but that doesn't make it a good idea.
key_insight: Algorithmic trading is far more accessible than most programmers assume—the core data structures and algorithms are straightforward, and the real barrier is the belief that finance is too complex to approach.
